---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: es-config
  namespace: elasticsearch
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    discovery.seed_hosts: es-headless
    cluster.initial_master_nodes: es-0,es-1,es-2
    xpack.security.enabled: "true"
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: "true"
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: "true"
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls/ca.crt
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-headless
  namespace: elasticsearch
  labels:
    app: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
  - name: transport
    port: 9300
  - name: http
    port: 9200
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-http
  namespace: elasticsearch
  labels:
    app: elasticsearch
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: elasticsearch
  ports:
  - name: https
    port: 9200
    targetPort: 9200
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: es
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  serviceName: es-headless
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: increase-vm-max-map
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
      - name: fix-permissions
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sh", "-c", "chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
      - name: elasticsearch
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        env:
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: elastic-password
              key: password
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9200
          name: http
        - containerPort: 9300
          name: transport
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: 1Gi
          limits:
            memory: 1Gi
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - |
              curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | \
                grep -qE '"status":"(green|yellow)"'
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 30
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        - name: config
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
          subPath: elasticsearch.yml
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: es-config
      - name: tls
        secret:
          secretName: es-http-tls
      - name: data
        emptyDir: {}

  if [ "$READY" -eq 3 ] && echo "$STATUS" | grep -v "0/1" | grep -q "1/1"; then
    echo "All 3 nodes Running and Ready!"

  fi
  sleep 10
done

content.thinking:
Only `es-0` is running and it's stuck at 0/1 (not ready). The other pods (es-1, es-2) haven't even started yet. This is because StatefulSets create pods sequentially - es-1 and es-2 won't be created until es-0 becomes ready. But es-0 can't become ready because the readiness probe requires the cluster health to be green/yellow, which requires at least 2/3 nodes to form quorum.

This is a chicken-and-egg problem with StatefulSets: es-0 won't pass readiness until the cluster forms, but es-1 and es-2 won't start until es-0 is ready.
I can break this deadlock a few ways - I could switch to parallel pod creation so all three start at once, relax the readiness probe to be less strict, or dig into the es-0 logs to see what's actually failing. Let me check those logs first to understand the root cause, then consider using `podManagementPolicy: Parallel` to let all pods initialize together instead of sequentially.

content.thinking:
The issue is:
1. `es-headless` hostname can't be resolved. This is likely because the headless service needs at least one pod to be Ready before the DNS entries are created, AND the pod needs to be labeled to be selected by the headless service.

Wait, actually in Kubernetes, headless service DNS entries are created for pods that are Ready. But the pods need to be ready first. This creates a chicken-and-egg situation.

Actually, for headless services in Kubernetes, DNS A records are created for ALL pods that are selected by the service AND have the pod's `ready` gates checked. By default, for headless services, only Ready pods are included in DNS.
I can solve this with `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` on the Service spec, which will publish DNS records for all selected pods regardless of their Ready status. I also need to switch to `podManagementPolicy: Parallel` so all three pods start simultaneously instead of sequentially, which should break the bootstrap deadlock. es-2 never start because they're waiting for es-0 to be ready. With both `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` and `podManagementPolicy: Parallel`, all three pods launch simultaneously and the headless service immediately returns all their IPs, allowing the cluster to form. Since `podManagementPolicy` is immutable on existing StatefulSets, I'll need to delete and recreate it to apply this change. Now I'll patch the headless service to allow publishing addresses for not-ready pods, delete the current StatefulSet, and redeploy it with parallel pod management so all three replicas can start simultaneously instead of sequentially.

content.text:
Two problems found:
1. `es-headless` DNS isn't resolvable until pods are Ready (chicken-and-egg)  
2. StatefulSet sequential policy means es-1/es-2 never start until es-0 is Ready

Fix: add `publishNotReadyAddresses: true` to the headless service + set `podManagementPolicy: Parallel` on the StatefulSet.

        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: increase-vm-max-map
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sysctl", "-w", "vm.max_map_count=262144"]
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
      - name: fix-permissions
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sh", "-c", "chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
      - name: elasticsearch
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        env:
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: elastic-password
              key: password
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9200
          name: http
        - containerPort: 9300
          name: transport
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: 1Gi
          limits:
            memory: 1Gi
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - |
              curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" \
                https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | \
                grep -qE '"status":"(green|yellow)"'
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 30
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        - name: config
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
          subPath: elasticsearch.yml
        - name: tls
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/tls
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: es-config
      - name: tls
        secret:
          secretName: es-http-tls
      - name: data
        emptyDir: {}

content.thinking:
This is very interesting! The logs show:
1. es-0 and es-2 are discovering each other and can form a 2-node quorum
2. They're successfully electing a master (`[2] nodes joined in term 1/3/6`)
3. But the election keeps failing with "node is no longer master for term X while handling publication"

This is a split-brain situation where:
- es-0 and es-2 discover each other (they're already connected on transport)
- They elect a master... but then fail to commit the cluster state

Wait, I think I understand. 

The problem is that only 2 out of 3 initial master nodes are joining, which technically meets the quorum requirement. But when both nodes try to become master simultaneously, they can't agree on who should commit the cluster state—each one keeps losing its master status mid-publication because the other node is also claiming to be master.

This cycling might be happening because es-1 is still starting up and hasn't connected to the transport layer yet, leaving es-0 and es-2 in a deadlock. Or it could be an SSL certificate issue—since both nodes are using the same certificate with `verification_mode: certificate`, there might be inconsistent hostname verification or certificate validation failures that keep dropping their connection.

Looking at those error messages more carefully, it seems like two nodes are trying to complete an election but then one loses its master status during the publication phase, which suggests a fundamental issue with how the nodes are authenticating or communicating with each other in the transport layer.

The repeating pattern with escalating term numbers points to a split-brain scenario where es-1 keeps rejoining and disrupting the election process. I should check the latest logs to see if the cluster has stabilized or if this cycle is still happening.

content.text: Nodes are discovering each other (es-0 and es-2 are connecting) but the master election is cycling — this is likely es-1 joining late and disrupting elections. Let me check the latest logs.
